responses: A yeast infection, or candidiasis, is a fungal infection caused by an overgrowth of the fungus Candida. It most commonly affects the vaginal area, but can also occur in the mouth (thrush), skin folds, or nail beds.
It is not considered a Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI), though it can sometimes be passed between partners. It is essentially an imbalance of the natural bacteria and yeast in your body.

patterns: Why do I keep getting yeast infections?
responses: Overgrowth is often triggered by antibiotic use (which kills 'good' bacteria), hormonal changes (pregnancy or birth control), uncontrolled diabetes, or a weakened immune system.
Warm, moist environments facilitate yeast growth. Wearing wet swimsuits or tight, non-breathable synthetic underwear for long periods can also contribute to an infection.

Common symptoms include intense itching and irritation, a burning sensation (especially during urination or intercourse), and a thick, white, odorless vaginal discharge that looks like cottage cheese.
You may also notice redness, swelling, or soreness of the affected area. Unlike bacterial infections, a typical yeast infection does not usually have a strong or 'fishy' odor.
Wear breathable cotton underwear and avoid tight-fitting pants. Change out of wet workout clothes or swimsuits immediately.
Avoid scented feminine products, douches, and bubble baths, as these can disrupt the natural pH balance. When using the bathroom, always wipe from front to back.

patterns: What to eat for yeast infection?
responses: Probiotic-rich foods like plain Greek yogurt (with live cultures), kefir, and sauerkraut can help restore the 'good' bacteria (Lactobacillus) that keep yeast in check.
Try to reduce high-sugar foods and refined carbohydrates, as yeast thrives on sugar. Staying hydrated also supports your body's natural defense mechanisms.

patterns: How to treat a yeast infection at home?
responses: Over-the-counter antifungal creams or suppositories (like Miconazole/Monistat) are common treatments. These usually come in 1, 3, or 7-day formulations.

For persistent infections, a doctor may prescribe a single oral dose of Fluconazole (Diflucan). WARNING: If your symptoms don't improve within a few days, or if you are pregnant, consult a healthcare provider before self-treating.

patterns: What to do after a yeast infection?
How to tell if a yeast infection is gone?
responses: Complete the full course of treatment even if symptoms disappear early. If you were using a cream, wait until the infection is fully cleared before having intercourse to avoid further irritation.
If you experience more than four yeast infections in a year, see a doctor to check for underlying conditions like blood sugar issues or to discuss a long-term suppression plan.
